title: Ignore Biomechanics At Your Peril
excerpt: So the corrugated is a really good example of this if you think about the origin and insertion of the corrugator that can completely change whether you're likely to get a medial brow drop after if you just treat the frontalis what's next after vectors so the next thing that i lea…

segments.text: so the corrugated is a really good example of this if you think about the origin and insertion of the corrugator that can completely change whether you're likely to get a medial brow drop after if you just treat the frontalis what's next after vectors so the next thing that i learned after vectors was this idea of biomechanics and i actually learned this because i have one leg that's slightly shorter than the other only by a centimeter um and i had a physiotherapist a really good assessment of me and it was one of those experiences where you think wow you know i've never thought of it like this before and you know she was looking at how much my muscles were extending in different places and what my flexibility was like and explaining why this was causing back pain this whole complex narrative which he put together explained it beautifully and i got it and i was like wow this is a complex system and the same thing applies in the face which is there are little differences between different people that amount to different results with the same treatment if you're not if you're trying to do a

blanket approach for everyone so one example would be the insert origin and insertion points so the angle at which a muscle is attached from the bone onto the skin can change what the net effect is on when you relax that muscle so the corrugate is a really good example of this if you think about the origin and insertion of the corrugator that can completely change whether you're likely to get a medial brow drop after if you just treat the frontalis so it's a common rule many people say is i never treat the frontalis without treating the glabellar complex or the corrugator and the reason for that is the majority of people have this downward vector so when they frown it pulls their eyebrows down not just together but not everyone's like that and if you look closely you'll find maybe 10 15 people where it's actually quite neutral so they don't have a downed vector they're just creating those elevenses and those people actually probably don't need to have their glabellar complex treated when they're having a toxin treatment but that comes from understanding the biomechanics so origin insertion angle changes the vectors across the face and

you can see this also with the size of the muscle but it's happening in all the areas that we look at so the same might be true around the modiolus and how that's connected to the jawline and to the midface that you're going to get subtly different um results in terms of when that when the face is dynamic they just simply you get it for the same muscle you get a slightly different movement that might impact on your treatment so you've got to understand the face in terms of those um as i say the biomechanics how can you diagnose someone has a different biomechanics someone else well you need to watch them moving so um it's very simple i actually the probably some of the best observation you make is when you're not asking them to move and you're just listening to them speak and another good example would be with non-surgical rhinoplasty it's always a good idea when you're talking to someone to watch if their nose is moving a lot so if you see someone who's got a curved nose and they've got a lot of movement so their depressor nasal muscle is powerfully pulling down the tip of their nose that is a diagnosis i make without asking them to move i observe it while i'm talking to them and it becomes one of the
treatment options i can deliver if they want a straighter nose
